Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - GUIDING REPRESENTATION LEARNING IN DEEP GEN ERATIVE MODELS WITH POLICY GRADIENTS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Variational Auto Encoder (VAE) provide an efficient latent space representation of complex data distributions which is learned in an unsupervised fashion. Using such a representation as input to Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches may reduce learning time, enable domain transfer or improve interpretability of the model. However, current state-of-the-art approaches that combine VAE with RL fail at learning good performing policies on certain RL domains. Typically, the VAE is pre-trained in isolation and may omit the embedding of task-relevant features due to insufficiencies of its loss. As a result, the RL approach can not successfully maximize the reward on these domains. Therefore, this paper investigates the issues of joint training approaches and explores incorporation of policy gradients from RL into the VAE’s latent space to find a task-specific latent space representation. We show that using pre-trained representations can lead to policies being unable to learn any rewarding behaviour in these environments. Subsequently, we introduce two types of models which overcome this deficiency by using policy gradients to learn the representation. Thereby the models are able to embed features into its representation that are crucial for performance on the RL task but would not have been learned with previous methods.

## 1 INTRODCTION

Reinforcement Learning (RL) gained much popularity in recent years by outperforming humans in games such as _Atari_ (Mnih et al. (2015)), _Go_ (Silver et al. (2016)) and _Starcraft 2_ (Vinyals et al. (2017)). These results were facilitated by combining novel machine learning techniques such as deep neural networks (LeCun et al. (2015)) with classical RL methods. The RL framework has shown to be quite flexible and has been applied successfully in many further domains, for example, robotics (Andrychowicz et al. (2020)), resource management (Mao et al. (2016)) or physiologically accurate locomotion (Kidzi´nski et al. (2018)).

The goal of representation learning is to learn a suitable representation for a given application domain. Such a representation should contain useful information for a particular downstream task and capture the distribution of explanatory factors (Bengio et al. (2013)). Typically, the choice of a downstream task influences the choice of method for representation learning. While Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)s are frequently used for tasks that require high-fidelity reconstructions or generation of realistic new data, auto-encoder based methods have been more common in RL. Recently, many such approaches employed the Variational Auto Encoder (VAE) (Kingma & Welling (2013)) framework which aims to learn a smooth representation of its domain.

Most of these approaches follow the same pattern: First, they build a dataset of states from the RL environment. Second, they train the VAE on this static dataset and lastly train the RL mode using the VAE’s representation. While this procedure generates sufficiently good results for certain scenarios, there are some fundamental issues with this method. Such an approach assumes that it is possible to collect enough data and observe all task-relevant states in the environment without knowing how to act in it. As a consequence, when learning to act the agent will only have access to a representation that is optimized for the known and visited states. As soon as the agent becomes more competent, it might experience novel states that have not been visited before and for which there is no good representation (in the sense that the experienced states are out of the original learned distribution and the mapping is not appropriate).

Another issue arises from the manner the representation is learned. Usually, the VAE is trained in isolation, so it decides what features are learned based on its own objective function and not on what is helpful for the downstream task. Mostly, such a model is tuned for good reconstruction. Without the information from the RL model, such a representation does not reflect what is important for the downstream task. As a consequence, the VAE might omit learning features that are crucial for good performance on the task because they appear negligible with respect to reconstruction (Goodfellow et al. (2016), Chapter 15, Figure 15.5). For example, small objects in pixel-space are ignored as they affect a reconstruction based loss only marginally. Thus, any downstream task using such a representation will have no access to information about such objects. A good example for such a task is Atari Breakout, a common RL benchmark. Figures 1a and 1b show an original Breakout frame and its reconstruction. While the original frame contains the ball in the lower right hand corner, this crucial feature is missing completely in the reconstruction.

We approach this issue through simultaneously learning representation and RL task, that is by combining the training of both models. As an advantage, this abolishes the need of collecting data before knowing the environment as it combines VAE and RL objectives. In consequence the VAE has an incentive to represent features that are relevant to the RL model. The main contributions of this paper are as follows: First we show that combined learning is possible and that it yields good performing policies. Second, we show that jointly trained representations incorporate additional, task-specific information which allows a RL agent to achieve higher rewards then if it was trained on a static representation. This will be shown indirectly by comparing achieved rewards as well as directly through an analysis of the trained model and its representation.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Lange & Riedmiller (2010) explored Auto Encoder (AE) (Lecun (1987); Bourlard & Kamp (1988); Hinton & Zemel (1994)) as a possible pre-processor for RL algorithms. The main focus in their work was finding good representations for high dimensional state spaces that enables policy learning. As input, rendered images from the commonly used grid world environment were used. The agent had to manoeuvre through a discretized map using one of four discrete movement actions per timestep. It received a positive reward once reaching the goal tile and negative rewards elsewhere. The AE bottleneck consisted only of two neurons, which corresponds to the dimensionality of the environemnt’s state. Fitted Q-Iteration (FQI) (Ernst et al. (2005)) was used to estimate the _Q_ -function, which the agent then acted _ϵ_ -greedy upon. Besides RL, they also used the learned representation to classify the agents position given an encoding using a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) (Rumelhart et al. (1985)). For these experiments, they found that adapting the encoder using MLP

gradients lead to an accuracy of 99 _._ 46 %. However, they did not apply this approach to their RL task.

A compelling example for separate training of meaningful representation is provided by Higgins et al. (2017b) who proposed a framework called _DARLA_ . They trained RL agents on the encoding of a _β_ -VAE (Higgins et al. (2016); Higgins et al. (2017a)) with the goal of zero-shot domain transfer. In their approach, _β_ -VAE and agent were trained separately on a source domain and then evaluated in a target domain. Importantly, source and target domain are similar to a certain extent and only differ in some features, e.g. a blue object in the source domain might be red in the target domain. During training of the _β_ -VAE, the pixel-based reconstruction loss was replaced with a loss calculated in the latent space of a Denoising Auto Encoder (DAE) (Vincent et al. (2008)). Thereby their approach avoids missing task relevant feature encodings at the cost of training another model. For one of their evaluation models, they allowed the RL gradients to adapt the encoder. Their results show that subsequent encoder learning improves performance of Deep Q-Learning (DQN) but decreases performance of Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic (A3C) (Mnih et al. (2016)).

Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) proposed a combination of VAE, Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)) and a simple policy as a controller. They hypothesized that by learning a good representation of the environment and having the ability to predict future states, learning the policy itself becomes a trivial task. Like in most other models, the VAE was pre-trained on data collected by a random policy. Only the RNN and the controller were trained online. The compressed representation from the VAE was passed into a RNN in order to estimate a probability density for the subsequent state. The controller was deliberately chosen as a single linear layer and could thus be optimized with Covariance Matrix Adaptation - Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES) (Hansen (2006)).

This work demonstrated how a VAE can provide a versatile representation that can be utilized in reinforcement learning. In addition, such an approach allows to predict the subsequent encoded state. While these findings encourage the usage of VAE in conjunction with RL, this is only possible in environments where the state space can be explored sufficiently by a random policy. However, if the policy can only discover important features after acquiring a minimal level of skill, sampling the state space using a random policy will not yield high-performing agents. Learning such features would only be possible if the VAE is continuously improved during policy training.

Another interesting combination of VAEs and RL was recently proposed by Yang et al. (2019), with their so called Action-Conditional Variational Auto-Encoder (AC-VAE). Their motivation for creating this model was to train a transparent, interpretable policy network. Usually, the _β_ -VAEs decoder is trained to reconstruct the input based on the representation the encoder produced. In this work though, the decoders objective was to predict the subsequent state _st_ +1. As input it got the latent space vector _**z**_ combined with an action-mapping-vector, which is the action vector _at_ with a zero-padding to match the latent spaces dimensionality. Inspecting the decoder estimates for _st_ +1 when varying one dimension of the latent space showed, that each dimension encoded a possible subsequent state that is likely to be encountered if the corresponding action from this dimension was taken. Unfortunately, the authors did not report any rewards they achieved on Breakout, hence it was not possible for us to compare model performances.

## 3 COMBINATION OF REINFORCEMENT AND REPRESENTATION LEARNING OBJECTIVES

In this section, we will first revisit the fundamentals of RL and VAEs and discuss their different objective functions. Then, we propose a joint objective function that allows for joint training of both models using gradient descent based learning methods.

## 3.1 REINFORCEMENT LEARNING WITH POLICY OPTIMIZATION

RL tries to optimize a Markov Decision Process (MDP) (Bellman (1957)) that is given by the tuple _⟨S, A, r, p, γ⟩_ . _S_ denotes the state space, _A_ the action space and _p_ : _S × R × S × A →_ [0 _,_ 1] the environment’s dynamics function that, provided a state-action pair, gives the state distribution for the successor state. _r_ : _S × A →R_ is the reward and _γ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1) the scalar discount factor. The policy _πθ_ ( _a|s_ ) is a stochastic function that gives a probability distribution over actions for state

## 3.2 LEARNING REPRESENTATIONS USING VARIATIONAL AUTO-ENCODERS

Kingma & Welling (2013) introduced the VAE as a method to perform Variational Inference (VI) (Jordan et al. (1999)) using function approximators, e.g. deep neural networks. VI tries to approximate a distribution over the generative factors of a dataset which would otherwise involve calculating an intractable integral. The authors present an algorithm that utilizes the auto encoder framework, an unsupervised learning method which learns data encodings by reconstructing its input. Therefore, the input is first compressed until it reaches a given size and is afterwards decompressed to its original size. When using deep neural networks, these transformations can be achieved by using for example fully connected or convolutional layers. In order for the VAE to approximate a distribution over generative factors, the authors used the so called ”reparametrization trick”. It allows for gradient based optimization methods to be used in searching for the distribution parameters. For training the VAE, a gradient based optimizer tries to minimize the following loss:

where _DKL_ denotes the Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KL) (Kullback & Leibler (1951)) of the approximated distribution over generative factors produced by the encoder _qφ_ ( _**z** |_ _**x**_ ) and some prior distribution _p_ ( _**z**_ ). The expectation is often referred to as reconstruction loss that is typically calculated on a per-pixel basis. Lastly, _l_ ( _**µ** ,_ _**σ** ,_ _**ϵ**_ ) is a sampling function that is differentiable w.r.t. the distribution parameters, for example _**z**_ = _**u**_ + _**σϵ**_ .

## 3.3 JOINT OBJECTIVE FUNCTION

Combining both loss functions such that both models can be trained at the same time is rather straight-forward. Adding both individual losses and using an optimizer such as ADAM (Kingma & Ba (2014)) to minimize them is sufficient to achieve joint training. During backpropagation, gradients from the policy and the VAE are combined in the latent space. Due to different topologies of the networks, gradient magnitudes differ significantly. Therefore, we introduced the hyperparameter _κ_ which can be used to either amplify or dampen the gradients and we arrive at the following loss:
where _L_[PG] is some policy gradient algorithm’s objective function. As mentioned before, we used PPO’s loss _L_[PPO] (equation 4 in the appendix).

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In order to test our model with the combined objective function given by equation 3, we have used the well-known benchmark of Atari Breakout. This environment has several properties that make it appealing to use: it is easily understandable by humans, used often as a RL task and the conventional pre-trained methods fail at mastering it. The ball is the most important feature that is required to be encoded in order to perform well, is heavily underrepresented (approximately 0.1% of the observation space). Therefore, the VAE’s incentive to encode it is very low whereas our model succeeds in encoding it. In the following, we compare the pre-trained approach to two different continuously trained models that use the loss from equation 3.

## 4.1 DATA COLLECTION AND PRE-PROCESSING

The raw RGB image data produced by the environment has a dimensionality of 210 _×_ 160 _×_ 3 pixels. We employ a similar pre-precessing as Mnih et al. (2015), but instead of cropping the greyscaled frames, we simply resize them to 84 _×_ 84 pixels. As we will first train models similar to those introduced in previous works with a pre-trained VAE, we needed to construct a dataset containing Breakout states. We used an already trained policy to collect a total of 25 _,_ 000 frames, the approximate equivalent of 50 episodes.

## 4.2 PRE-TRAINING THE VARIATIONAL AUTO-ENCODER

Our first model is based on those of the previously introduced works which involve isolated pretraining the VAE on a static dataset. Figure 2 shows the individual parts of the complete training process. For the first model, PPO[fixed] , the encoder and decoder (shown in orange and red) are pretrained before policy training. During this phase, there is no influence from the RL loss. Once the VAE training is finished, the decoder shown in red in Figure 2 is discarded completely. Later during

policy training, we use _n_ instances of the same encoder with shared weights that receive a sequence of the last _n_ frames as input. Stacking allows us to incorporate temporal information and for the policy to predict the ball’s trajectory. By sharing the weights, we ensure that the resulting encodings originate from the same function. **U** then represents the concatenated encodings of the sequence. This weight sharing method has proven to be more time efficient than to query the encoder _n_ times and concatenate the results afterwards.

Prior to policy training, we trained the VAE on the dataset we have collected before, with hyperparameters from table 1. Once pre-training was finished, we discarded the decoder weights and used the stacked encoder as input for the policy MLP. The MLP was then trained 10M steps with hyperparameters from table 2. During this training, the encoder weights were not changed by gradient updates anymore but remained fixed.

The second model we introduce is called PPO[adapt] , which has the same structure and hyperparameters as the first model. For this model, we also train the VAE in isolation first, however the encoder weights are not fixed anymore during policy training. Gradients from the RL objective are back propagated through the encoder, allowing it to learn throughout policy training. We hypothesize that features that are important for policy performance can be incorporated in an already learned representation.

Figure 3 compares the median rewards of three rollouts with different random seeds for all models. PPO[fixed] was not once able to achieve a reward of 10 or higher, while PPO[adapt] steadily improved its performance with final rewards well over 50. The learning curve of PPO[adapt] shows that the model is able to learn how to act in the environment, whereas PPO[fixed] does not. The non-zero rewards from PPO[fixed] are similar to those of random agents in Breakout. From these results, we can assume that training the VAE in isolation on a static dataset for Breakout results in a deficient representation for RL. Therefore, using policy gradients to adapt an already learned representation can be beneficial in environments where the VAE fails to encode task-relevant features.

already trained agent that gathers a dataset for the VAE beforehand is not necessary. The decoder is trained exactly as in the isolated setting, meaning its gradients are also only computed using the VAE’s loss function. During backpropagation, the gradients coming from **Z** and _h_ 1 are added together and passed through the encoder. This model has the same network configuration and hyperparameters as the first two, with the only difference that we also evaluated different values for _κ_ from the joint loss equation 3 (see A.3). For the results reported here, we chose _κ_ = 20. All hyperparameters can be found in table 3.

By simultaneously training representation and policy, we expect the VAE to learn task-relevant features from the beginning of training. This assumption is supported by the learning curve shown in figure 3, which compares PPO[VAE] to the previous two models. The curve shows a steady increase in reward over the course of training with PPO[VAE] achieving slightly higher rewards than PPO[adapt] in the beginning. This characteristic changes after less than 1M steps and from that point on PPO[adapt] consistently outperforms PPO[VAE] . This difference in performance is likely attributed to the fact, that in PPO[VAE] the decoder is trained throughout the complete training. While for PPO[adapt] , the latent space can be changed without restrictions, the decoder of PPO[VAE] constantly produces gradients do not contain information about the ball. Therefore, PPO[VAE] has less incentive to achieve higher rewards, which is reflected by its performance.

## 4.4 ANALYZING THE VALUE FUNCTION GRADIENTS

So far, the results imply that PPO[VAE] and PPO[adapt] do indeed learn encodings of the ball. One difficulty when analyzing the representation is, that the decoder still has no incentive to reconstruct the ball, even if it is present in the latent space. In a work that enhances DQN algorithm (Wang et al. (2016b)), the authors visualized the Jacobian of the value function w.r.t. the input images. These visualizations showed which features or regions from the input space are considered as important in terms of future reward. As we also learn a value function, we did the same and visualized what our model considered important and what not.

In figure 4 we illustrate a pre-processed frame and added the values of the Jacobian to the blue channel if the were greater than the mean value of the Jacobian. Only visualizing above-mean Jacobian values removes some noise in the blue channel makes the images much easier to interpret and only highlights regions of high relevance. We can clearly see, that the Jacobian has high values at missing blocks as well as around the ball, meaning that these regions are considered to have high impact on future rewards. By visualizing the Jacobian we have confirmed that the policy gradients encourage the VAE to embed task-relevant features.
Figure 4: The Jacobian of PPO’s value function. Highlighted areas mean high importance in terms of future rewards. Note the high Jacobian values around the ball and the blocks.

## 5 CONCLUSION

This paper focused on the issue of pre-training VAEs with the purpose of learning a policy for a downstream task based on the VAE’s representation. In many environments, the VAE has little to no incentive to learn task-relevant features if they are small in observation space. Another issue arises if the observation of these features depends on policy performance and as a result, they are underrepresented in a dataset sampled by a random agent. In both cases, fixing encoder weights during policy training prevents the VAE to learn these important features and policy performance will be underwhelming.

We carried out experiments on the popular RL benchmark Atari Breakout. The goal was to analyze whether policy gradients guide representation learning towards incorporating performancecritic features that a VAE would not learn on a pre-recorded dataset. First experiments confirmed, that the common pre-trained approach did not yield well-performing policies in this environment. Allowing the policy gradients to adapt encoder weights in two different models showed significant

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

improvements in terms of rewards. With policy gradients guiding the learned representation, agents consistently outperformed those that were trained on a fixed representation.

Our work verifies that the fundamental issue with pre-trained representations still exists and shows possible solutions in RL scenarios. Nonetheless, future work can still explore a variety of improvements to our models. For once, training not only the encoder but also the decoder with RL gradients can improve interpretability of the VAE and enable it to be used as a generator again that also generates task-relevant features. Another direction is to impose further restrictions on the latent space during joint training of VAE and policy. The goal there would be to maintain the desired latent space characteristics of VAEs while still encoding task-relevant features.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a well-motivated and significant challenge in deep reinforcement learning: the misalignment between unsupervised representation learning (e.g., VAEs) and downstream task performance. The introduction is highly effective, providing a clear, intuitive justification for joint training by highlighting the "small object" problem—where reconstruction losses ignore features that are vital for RL success. The technical motivation is strong, and the proposed approach of using policy gradients to bridge the gap between latent representation and reward optimization is theoretically sound and well-structured. However, the manuscript would benefit from a more rigorous definition of the proposed architectures in the transition from the introduction to the methodology. While the central premise is compelling, the paper must ensure that the empirical evaluation does not just demonstrate *that* the models work, but explicitly isolates *how* the policy gradient signal shapes the latent space compared to standard VAE priors. Overall, the foundational framing is excellent, and the paper is well-positioned to contribute a necessary refinement to current representation learning practices in RL.

comments.title: Missing formal definitions of objective functions
comments.quote: For training the VAE, a gradient based optimizer tries to minimize the following loss: ... and we arrive at the following loss: where $L_{PG}$ is some policy gradient algorithm’s objective function.
comments.explanation: The paper refers to 'the following loss' multiple times for both the VAE and the joint RL/VAE objective, but the mathematical expressions are missing from the text. Without the formal definition of the loss functions (e.g., how the hyperparameters like $\kappa$ are applied), the methodology is underspecified, hindering reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity in MDP objective formulation
comments.quote: The overarching goal of RL is to find a policy that maximizes the average collected reward over all trajectories.
comments.explanation: The RL objective is presented with inconsistent and garbled notation (e.g., missing indices for summation, absence of the discount factor $\gamma$, and unclear definition of the return $r(\tau)$). For a technical paper in reinforcement learning, the objective function should be presented with standard, well-defined notation.

comments.title: Unsupported causal claims regarding training dynamics
comments.quote: the decoder of PPO[VAE] constantly produces gradients [that] do not contain information about the ball.
comments.explanation: The authors make a strong causal claim about the failure of PPO[VAE] being due to 'decoder gradients' and later claim that Jacobian visualization 'confirms' the policy gradients' role in feature emergence. These interpretations are presented without empirical validation (e.g., ablation studies, gradient magnitude analysis), leaving them as speculative rather than confirmed results.

comments.title: Lack of justification for sensitivity and design of joint objective weighting
comments.quote: L = L[PG] + \kappa * Loss[VAE]
comments.explanation: The paper introduces a hyperparameter $\kappa$ to weight the multi-objective loss but does not provide a formal justification for the scalarization or discuss the sensitivity of the training stability to this hyperparameter. Given that this is a critical component for joint representation learning, the lack of sensitivity analysis or selection criteria is a deficiency in the methodology.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of gradient backpropagation and visualization
comments.quote: During backpropagation, the gradients coming from Z and h1 are added together...
comments.explanation: The description relies on undefined variables (e.g., '$h_1$') and ambiguous processing steps (e.g., whether the Jacobian visualization is an additive overlay or a thresholded replacement). These inaccuracies make the implementation details difficult to follow.

comments.title: Definition ambiguity in environment and state space

comments.explanation: The text conflates the raw input space (images) with the abstract state space of the environment. While the distinction may be colloquially understood, in a technical paper, it is important to clarify that the AE bottleneck models an abstract representation rather than the 'environment state' directly.

comments.title: Inconsistencies in professional polish

comments.explanation: There are several minor proofreading errors, including spacing in the title, typos in section headers, and grammatical slips in the body text. These issues reflect a lack of final polish in the manuscript.

Given that $PPO_{VAE}$ is specifically designed to learn representations relevant to the task, this failure is likely due to balancing issues between the VAE loss and the RL loss, not necessarily a lack of information in the gradients themselves.

comments.title: Inconsistent formatting and unclear definition of the Jacobian visualization

comments.explanation: The text states: 'if the were greater than the mean value'. It is clear the authors meant 'if they were'. Furthermore, the phrasing 'if the [values] were greater than the mean value' implies a thresholding operation (setting values to zero if below the mean), but the subsequent sentence 'removes some noise... makes the images much easier to interpret' suggests this is a post-processing or masking step. A precise technical description should clarify if the blue channel value reflects the raw gradient magnitude, a masked gradient, or a normalized gradient. While the intent is clear, the minor grammatical error ('the' vs 'they') combined with the imprecise description of the visualization processing (is it an additive overlay or a replacement?) is slightly ambiguous for a methodology section reporting experimental results.

comments.title: Potential overstatement of evidence in Jacobian analysis
comments.quote: By visualizing the Jacobian we have confirmed that the policy gradients encourage the VAE to embed task-relevant features.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that this visualization 'confirms' that policy gradients encourage the VAE to embed task-relevant features. While the visualization shows correlation—high gradients around the ball—it is a post-hoc analysis of the model once trained. To fully 'confirm' that the policy gradients are the mechanism of encouragement, one would ideally need to show that the features are absent when gradients are disabled (already shown) and explicitly present when active. The claim is slightly strong given that the Jacobian of the value function reflects the agent's current 'attention' but does not prove the causal mechanism of the feature emergence in the encoder's internal weights. It is a reasonable inference, but framing it as a 'confirmation' of the learning incentive is a slight overclaim.

The summation index $t$ is missing its bounds, and the expression $\sum_{t}[r]([s, a])$ is notationally disconnected from the trajectory $\tau$ defined in the same paragraph as $(s_0, a_0, ..., s_T, a_T)$. Furthermore, the text later correctly references Equation (1) with a sum from $t=0$ to $T$. While the intent is clear as a reference to the cumulative reward, the notation $\sum_{t}[r]([s, a])$ is imprecise and potentially confusing for readers expecting standard RL notation (usually $\sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t r_t$).

comments.explanation: Checking Equation (1) in Section 3.1, the authors write the gradient of the objective. Standard policy gradient theorem includes a sum over time steps and often uses the return $G_t$ or advantage $A_t$ rather than just $r(\tau)$. The provided formula is a simplified form (REINFORCE), which is valid, but the notation $\log \pi_{\theta}(\tau)$ is shorthand for $\sum \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t|s_t)$. Given the earlier definition of $\tau$ as a sequence, this is acceptable as an overview, but the inclusion of $r(\tau)$ directly inside the gradient expectation without explicitly noting it as a cumulative return is a slight mathematical gap that could confuse a student reader.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Joint Loss' definition vs implementation
comments.quote: L = L[PG] + \kappa * Loss[VAE]
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, it is stated that adding individual losses and using ADAM is 'sufficient' for joint training. However, the authors note that gradients from the policy and VAE have different magnitudes and introduce $\kappa$. The paper does not provide a formal justification for the scalarization of these two distinct objectives (multi-objective optimization). While the authors mention they used $\kappa = 20$ in Section 4.3, they do not explain the criteria for selecting this weighting or how sensitivity to $\kappa$ influences stability, which is a common pitfall in joint RL/VAE training.

comments.title: Unsupported Claim regarding Decoder Gradients

comments.explanation: In Section 4.3, the authors claim the decoder's gradients hinder PPO[VAE] performance because they 'do not contain information about the ball.' This is a strong claim that contradicts their own premise that joint training should allow the VAE latitude to learn latent representations. If the latent space successfully encodes the 'ball' feature (as they claim elsewhere), the decoder *must* be capable of reconstructing it if the reconstruction loss is to be minimized. Attributing the lower performance solely to the 'decoder gradients' without specific diagnostic validation of why those gradients are counter-productive is a significant interpretative gap.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a clearly motivated and practically relevant problem: the frequent misalignment between reconstruction-optimized VAE pre-training and the information needs of downstream reinforcement learning, particularly the systematic omission of task-critical but visually subtle features. The introduction is generally well-written, logically structured, and effectively uses concrete examples (e.g., Atari Breakout) to build intuition. However, the manuscript requires notable refinement to meet top-tier conference standards. First, the novelty and technical contribution are under-specified; while joint representation-policy learning has been explored in prior literature (e.g., RL-VAE, policy-guided autoencoders, and latent-space RL), the paper does not precisely delineate how its two proposed model variants differ algorithmically or where they outperform established baselines. Second, clarity and rigor would benefit from immediate attention to technical precision and proofreading: recurring typographical errors, inconsistent capitalization (“GEN ERATIVE,” “INTODCTION”), missing hyphens (“reconstruction based loss”), and grammatical slips undermine professional presentation, and the related work section cuts off mid-sentence, suggesting incomplete preparation. Finally, the authors should provide explicit algorithmic formulations and training objectives before transitioning to empirical claims. With stronger positioning relative to existing joint training approaches, polished writing, and precise methodological descriptions, this work could make a solid contribution.

comments.title: Ambiguous and underspecified backpropagation description with undefined symbols
comments.quote: During backpropagation, the gradients coming from **Z** and _h_ 1 are added together and passed through the encoder.
comments.explanation: The description of joint training backpropagation is imprecise and lacks reproducibility. The symbols **Z** and _h_1 appear without definition. **Z** typically denotes the stochastic latent variable in VAEs, not a loss component or network module generating gradients; it likely conflates the sample with the deterministic encoding (often **U** or **\mu**). Similarly, _h_1 likely refers to a hidden layer of the policy network but is never specified. The exact mechanism for routing and summing these gradients (e.g., in loss space vs. latent space, handling of gradient magnitudes via _\kappa_) is left vague. This ambiguity hinders implementation and violates standard notational conventions.

comments.title: Insufficient and logically flawed justification for performance gap
comments.quote: This difference in performance is likely attributed to the fact, that in PPO[VAE] the decoder is trained throughout the complete training. While for PPO[adapt] , the latent space can be changed without restrictions, the decoder of PPO[VAE] constantly produces gradients do not contain information about the ball. Therefore, PPO[VAE] has less incentive to achieve higher rewards, which is reflected by its performance.
comments.explanation: The authors claim PPO[VAE] underperforms PPO[adapt] because the decoder's reconstruction gradients 'do not contain information about the ball,' leading to less incentive for rewards. This reasoning is logically flawed and poorly articulated (notable grammatical errors). Joint training inherently backpropagates policy gradients through the encoder, which should directly incentivize encoding task-relevant features regardless of the decoder's output. The text fails to explain why reconstruction gradients would actively hinder ball encoding, and omits discussion of likely culprits like gradient conflict between reconstruction and RL objectives, optimization instability, or interference effects. The claim lacks theoretical or empirical backing.

comments.title: Contradiction in MDP dynamics definition and notation
comments.quote: _p_ : _S \times R \times S \times A \to_ [0 _,_ 1] the environment’s dynamics function that, provided a state-action pair, gives the state distribution for the successor state.
comments.explanation: The mathematical definition assigns a domain of `S \times R \times S \times A` to the dynamics function `p`, implying four inputs. However, the accompanying prose explicitly states it operates 'provided a state-action pair', creating a direct contradiction. Furthermore, `R` is defined later as the reward space, which has no theoretical role in defining state transition probabilities. This is likely a typographical error (intended: `S \times A \to [0, 1]`), but as written it introduces an irrelevant/undefined variable into the dynamics function and misaligns the formal notation with the textual explanation.

comments.title: Unjustified preprocessing choice violates Markov property
comments.quote: We employ a similar pre-precessing as Mnih et al. (2015), but instead of cropping the greyscaled frames, we simply resize them to 84 _\times_ 84 pixels.
comments.explanation: The authors state they resize frames to 84x84 instead of cropping them as in the standard Mnih et al. (2015) Atari setup. Standard preprocessing explicitly crops out static UI elements (score, lives) to preserve the Markov property. By simply resizing, these non-environment elements remain, violating the Markov assumption fundamental to RL. The paper omits any discussion, justification, or analysis of this methodological deviation's potential impact on training dynamics, policy convergence, or reward estimation. This undermines the theoretical grounding and reproducibility of the experimental setup.

comments.title: Underspecified gradient combination mechanism and loss weighting
comments.quote: During backpropagation, gradients from the policy and the VAE are combined in the latent space. Due to different topologies of the networks, gradient magnitudes differ significantly. Therefore, we introduced the hyperparameter _\kappa_ which can be used to either amplify or dampen the gradients and we arrive at the following loss:
comments.explanation: The authors introduce a scaling hyperparameter _\kappa_ to handle gradient magnitude differences between the VAE and policy objectives, but the explicit mathematical formulation is missing. The surrounding text does not clarify whether _\kappa_ weights the VAE term or the RL term (e.g., `L_joint = L_VAE + \kappa L_PG` vs `L_joint = \kappa L_VAE + L_PG`), nor does it specify the aggregation strategy. Standard practice in joint representation-learning makes this weighting explicit. Without it, readers cannot confidently reconstruct the training loop or tune _\kappa_.

comments.title: Unexplained reward scale and threshold in performance claims
comments.quote: PPO[fixed] was not once able to achieve a reward of 10 or higher, while PPO[adapt] steadily improved its performance with final rewards well over 50.
comments.explanation: The paper claims PPO[fixed] failed to achieve a reward of 10, while PPO[adapt] reached over 50. This threshold appears unusually low compared to standard Atari Breakout benchmarks (where canonical scores often exceed 500+). The authors do not specify the reward function, clipping, or scaling convention used. This omission makes the severity of the failure ambiguous and hinders comparison with established VAE+RL literature. Clarifying the reward formulation or noting its relation to the canonical Breakout setup is necessary.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of backpropagation gradients
comments.quote: During backpropagation, the gradients coming from **Z** and _h_ 1 are added together and passed through the encoder.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the phrase 'gradients coming from **Z** and _h_ 1'. **Z** is consistently used throughout the paper to denote the latent variable/sampling space, not a loss component or network module that would generate gradients during backpropagation. _h_ 1 is never defined in the text. I checked the model description and assumed **Z** might loosely refer to the VAE decoder loss and _h_ 1 to a hidden layer of the policy network, but even with this charitable reading, the terminology is imprecise. Standard joint training backpropagates the sum of the VAE reconstruction loss and the RL policy gradient. As written, a reproducer might be uncertain whether 'Z' refers to latent activations, sampling gradients, or the decoder loss, and 'h1' remains undefined. This ambiguity hinders clear implementation of the backward pass and violates standard notation conventions.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for PPO[VAE] underperformance
comments.quote: This difference in performance is likely attributed to the fact, that in PPO[VAE] the decoder is trained throughout the complete training. While for PPO[adapt] , the latent space can be changed without restrictions, the decoder of PPO[VAE] constantly produces gradients do not contain information about the ball. Therefore, PPO[VAE] has less incentive to achieve higher rewards, which is reflected by its performance.
comments.explanation: The authors claim PPO[VAE] underperforms PPO[adapt] because the decoder's gradients lack ball information, leading to 'less incentive to achieve higher rewards.' I initially questioned this because joint training inherently backpropagates RL gradients through the encoder, which should provide a direct incentive to encode task-relevant features like the ball, regardless of the decoder's reconstruction quality. I verified the joint loss formulation (Section 3.3) and the training procedure, confirming that both objectives contribute gradients simultaneously. The stated reason appears logically flawed or poorly articulated; the underperformance is more likely due to gradient conflict between reconstruction and RL objectives, or optimization instability from training the decoder end-to-end with the policy. Furthermore, the sentence contains a grammatical error ('gradients do not contain') that obscures the intended mechanism. The claim that combining objectives inherently provides 'less incentive' contradicts the paper's core premise and lacks sufficient empirical or theoretical backing.

comments.title: Preprocessing choice violates Markov property without discussion
comments.quote: We employ a similar pre-precessing as Mnih et al. (2015), but instead of cropping the greyscaled frames, we simply resize them to 84 _×_ 84 pixels.
comments.explanation: The authors state they resize frames to 84x84 instead of cropping them as in the standard Mnih et al. (2015) setup. I initially flagged this because standard Atari preprocessing explicitly crops out the score, lives, and other static UI elements to preserve the Markov property. By simply resizing, the observation retains these non-environment elements, which introduces a violation of the Markov assumption fundamental to RL. I checked the rest of the paper to see if they discussed this or provided a reason for the change, but found no justification or analysis of its potential impact on training dynamics, policy convergence, or reward estimation. While simplifying preprocessing is understandable, omitting discussion of this critical methodological deviation undermines the theoretical grounding and reproducibility of their experimental setup.

comments.title: Contradiction in MDP dynamics definition
comments.quote: _p_ : _S × R × S × A →_ [0 _,_ 1] the environment’s dynamics function that, provided a state-action pair, gives the state distribution for the successor state.
comments.explanation: The mathematical definition assigns a domain of `S × R × S × A` to the dynamics function `p`, implying it takes four inputs. However, the accompanying prose explicitly states it operates 'provided a state-action pair', creating a direct contradiction between the formal notation and the textual explanation. Furthermore, `R` is defined later in the paper as the reward space (`r : S × A → R`), which has no theoretical role in defining state transition probabilities. This is almost certainly a typographical error (likely meant to be `S × A → [0, 1]`), but as written it introduces an irrelevant/undefined variable into the dynamics and misaligns the prose with the formula.

comments.title: Underspecified gradient backpropagation in joint training
comments.quote: During backpropagation, the gradients coming from **Z** and _h_ 1 are added together and passed through the encoder.
comments.explanation: The description of how joint gradients are combined for `PPO[VAE]` lacks the precision needed for reproducibility. `**Z**` denotes the stochastically sampled latent vector, but policy gradients typically flow through the reparameterized mean `**U**` (as established in Figure 2's caption and standard VAE practice). Additionally, `h_1` is referenced without any prior definition; it likely refers to the first hidden layer of the policy MLP, but this is never specified. The exact mechanism for routing gradients from a policy hidden layer back to the VAE encoder, and how they are mathematically summed with the decoder's reconstruction gradients, is left vague. This ambiguity could mislead a reader attempting to implement the exact backward pass.

comments.title: Underspecified gradient combination mechanism
comments.quote: During backpropagation, gradients from the policy and the VAE are combined in the latent space. Due to different topologies of the networks, gradient magnitudes differ significantly. Therefore, we introduced the hyperparameter _κ_ which can be used to either amplify or dampen the gradients and we arrive at the following loss:
comments.explanation: I initially wondered how two distinct loss functions (VAE reconstruction/regularization and RL policy objective) are mathematically fused during a single optimization step. The authors introduce a scaling hyperparameter _κ_ to handle gradient magnitude differences, but the explicit formulation is missing from the OCR extraction. Even assuming the equation exists in the manuscript, the surrounding prose does not clarify whether _κ_ weights the VAE term or the RL term (e.g., _L_{joint} = L_{VAE} + κ L_{PG}_ vs _L_{joint} = κ L_{VAE} + L_{PG}_), nor does it specify the aggregation strategy (e.g., direct summation, gradient clipping, or normalization). Standard practice in joint representation-learning objectives typically makes this weighting explicit. Without it, a reader cannot confidently reconstruct the training loop or tune _κ_ for different environments.

comments.title: Undefined symbols in backpropagation description
comments.quote: During backpropagation, the gradients coming from **Z** and _h_ 1 are added together and passed through the encoder.
comments.explanation: While tracing the computational graph for the jointly trained model, I noted the sudden appearance of **Z** and _h_ 1. Neither is defined in the provided context. Standard VAE notation uses lowercase _z_ for the stochastic latent variable and _U_ or _μ_ for the deterministic mean/encoding. The bold **Z** might be a typographical holdover or refer to a latent-space loss, while _h_ 1 likely denotes a hidden layer of the policy MLP, but this is never stated. Because gradient routing depends on precise layer/variable identities, undefined symbols create ambiguity about where exactly the policy and VAE gradients intersect before reaching the encoder. Assuming _Z_ was intended to be _U_ (the concatenated encodings), the sentence would still need clarification on whether the addition occurs at the latent node or in loss space, but as written it is unclear.

comments.title: Ambiguous causal explanation for performance gap
comments.quote: This difference in performance is likely attributed to the fact, that in PPO[VAE] the decoder is trained throughout the complete training. While for PPO[adapt] , the latent space can be changed without restrictions, the decoder of PPO[VAE] constantly produces gradients do not contain information about the ball.
comments.explanation: The grammatical break ('produces gradients do not contain') initially obscured the claim, but even after parsing it, the logical reasoning feels under-justified. The authors suggest that because the decoder's reconstruction gradients lack ball-relevant signal, the jointly trained model underperforms the adaptively fine-tuned one. However, if the encoder receives *both* VAE and policy gradients (as stated in Section 4.3), the policy gradient should theoretically act as a corrective force that overrides or supplements the reconstruction signal. The text does not explain why reconstruction gradients would actively hinder ball encoding rather than merely providing neutral noise, nor does it address potential interference effects like catastrophic forgetting or conflicting update directions. A more rigorous explanation would reference how unsupervised reconstruction loss dominates or misaligns with the sparse RL reward signal during joint optimization.

comments.title: Unexplained reward scale in failure claim
comments.quote: PPO[fixed] was not once able to achieve a reward of 10 or higher, while PPO[adapt] steadily improved its performance with final rewards well over 50.
comments.explanation: I cross-referenced the stated reward thresholds with standard Atari Breakout benchmarks, where the reward typically corresponds to the in-game score (often reaching 500+ for even basic play). Claiming a maximum of 10 implies either severe reward clipping, a heavily scaled surrogate reward, or a non-standard environment wrapper. While the authors mention using a custom 25,000-frame dataset and pre-processing, they never specify the reward function or scaling convention. This leaves the severity of the pre-trained model's failure ambiguous and makes it difficult to compare their results with established VAE+RL literature. Clarifying the reward formulation or noting if it matches the canonical Breakout setup would resolve this ambiguity.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
### High-Level Feedback

The paper addresses an important and timely problem—the misalignment between unsupervised representation learning and downstream reinforcement learning tasks—and proposes a promising solution by integrating policy gradients into VAE training. However, the manuscript suffers from several significant issues that undermine its clarity and impact. First, the writing contains numerous typographical and grammatical errors (e.g., “INTRODCTION,” “environemnt’s,” missing figures) that distract from the technical content and suggest insufficient proofreading. Second, the claims regarding the VAE’s failure to capture small but task-relevant features (e.g., the ball in Breakout) are compelling but lack quantitative support; the paper would benefit from a systematic analysis or ablation study demonstrating that the proposed joint training indeed recovers such features. Third, the related work discussion is somewhat superficial, failing to clearly differentiate the proposed method from existing joint training approaches (e.g., Higgins et al.’s DARLA, which already uses a β-VAE but trains separately) or to address why simpler fine-tuning strategies would not suffice. Finally, the abstract and introduction promise two model types but do not describe them even at a high level, leaving the reader without a clear sense of the architectural innovation. Overall, while the core idea is worthwhile, the paper requires substantial revision to meet the clarity and rigor expected at a top conference.

comments.title: Missing equation for joint objective function
comments.quote: where L_[PG] is some policy gradient algorithm's objective function. As mentioned before, we used PPO's loss L_[PPO] (equation 4 in the appendix).
comments.explanation: Section 3.3 is titled 'JOINT OBJECTIVE FUNCTION' and is central to the paper's contribution. The text states 'we arrive at the following loss:' but no equation appears in the extracted passage. The reader cannot determine the exact form of the combined loss, how the hyperparameter κ is applied, or how gradients are combined. This is a critical omission that undermines the understanding of the method. Even assuming an OCR extraction error, the missing equation is a real issue that prevents evaluation of the mathematical correctness.

comments.title: Undefined symbol h_1 in gradient combination description
comments.quote: During backpropagation, the gradients coming from Z and h_1 are added together and passed through the encoder.
comments.explanation: The symbol h_1 is not defined in the passage or in the provided context (including Figure 2 and earlier sections). It likely refers to some hidden layer or intermediate representation in the policy network, but its origin and role are ambiguous. A reader cannot determine which gradients are being combined or how the flow is structured. This ambiguity could lead to incorrect interpretation of the joint training architecture.

comments.title: Missing definition of 'n' in architecture description
comments.quote: n instances of the same encoder with shared weights that receive a sequence of the last n frames as input.
comments.explanation: The variable 'n' is used to describe stacking multiple frames, but its value is never explicitly stated in the provided context. It might appear later in hyperparameter tables (not shown). Without the value, the method is underspecified for reproducibility. However, given it's a common technique (e.g., n=4 in DQN for Atari), the omission is mitigated by convention. Still, a reader would need to infer or find the value elsewhere.

comments.title: Overly strong claim in abstract: 'can not successfully maximize the reward on these domains'
comments.quote: the RL approach can not successfully maximize the reward on these domains
comments.explanation: The claim that the RL approach 'can not successfully maximize the reward' is quite strong. The paper later shows that pre-trained VAE leads to very low rewards (around 10 or less) on Breakout, which is consistent. However, the phrasing suggests a universal impossibility, whereas the paper only demonstrates failure on certain domains (Breakout). The context of 'certain RL domains' partially mitigates this, but the claim could be seen as overreaching. Acceptable in an abstract for brevity.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about PPO[VAE] having 'less incentive to achieve higher rewards'
comments.quote: PPO[VAE] has less incentive to achieve higher rewards, which is reflected by its performance.
comments.explanation: The explanation for why PPO[VAE] performs worse than PPO[adapt] is that 'the decoder of PPO[VAE] constantly produces gradients do not contain information about the ball. Therefore, PPO[VAE] has less incentive to achieve higher rewards.' This is logically questionable: the policy loss still provides gradients that incentivize reward maximization. The decoder loss merely adds additional gradients that may conflict or dilute the policy signal. The phrasing anthropomorphizes the model ('less incentive') and oversimplifies the interaction. This is a minor overclaim that conflates the effect of additional gradients with reduced incentive.

comments.title: Underspecified computation of Jacobian visualization
comments.quote: In figure 4 we illustrate a pre-processed frame and added the values of the Jacobian to the blue channel if the were greater than the mean value of the Jacobian.
comments.explanation: The description of the Jacobian visualization leaves several choices implicit. The value function receives a concatenation of encoded latent vectors from a stack of n frames (as described in Section 4.2). It is unclear whether the Jacobian is computed with respect to a single frame, all frames in the stack (and then one frame is shown), or the mean over frames. The thresholding criterion ('greater than the mean value of the Jacobian') is also ambiguous: is the mean taken over all pixels? Over the entire Jacobian tensor? Novice readers might misinterpret which input the gradient is taken w.r.t., and reproducibility is hindered. The authors likely used a standard saliency-map technique, but the description omits these details.

comments.title: Possible misattribution of prior work on Jacobian visualization
comments.quote: In a work that enhances DQN algorithm (Wang et al. (2016b)), the authors visualized the Jacobian of the value function w.r.t. the input images.
comments.explanation: The cited work Wang et al. (2016b) is the Dueling Network Architectures paper. While that paper does visualise the value and advantage functions, I do not recall it presenting Jacobian (gradient) heatmaps w.r.t. input pixels. The more common technique for such visualizations in RL is to use gradient-based saliency (e.g., from the DQN paper or later works). This could be a minor inaccuracy that does not affect the paper's own contribution, but it might mislead readers about what prior work actually demonstrated. The reference should be checked; if it is indeed incorrect, a correction would improve clarity.

The later claim that PPO[VAE] has 'less incentive' seems to imply the joint training is detrimental, yet the experiment shows PPO[VAE] still achieves decent rewards (~35 median?). comments.title: Nonstandard MDP dynamics function notation
comments.quote: p : S × R × S × A → [0, 1] the environment’s dynamics function that, provided a state-action pair, gives the state distribution for the successor state.
comments.explanation: The notation writes the dynamics function with domain S × R × S × A, but the accompanying text says that given a state-action pair, it gives the distribution over the successor state. The standard notation for a Markov decision process typically defines the transition dynamics as p(s', r | s, a) or p(s' | s, a) with a separate reward function. Here the product includes the current state, reward, next state, and action, which is ambiguous and does not clearly indicate which variables are conditioned. A careful reader may be unsure whether the function is meant to represent the joint probability of the tuple (s, r, s', a) or the conditional probability p(s', r | s, a). The prose focuses on the successor state distribution, which suggests the reward is part of the output, so the domain notation should reflect the conditioning variables (s, a) rather than including all four variables. This is a minor notational inconsistency that could mislead someone trying to formalize the MDP.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling argument for addressing the fundamental misalignment between unsupervised representation learning and the goal-oriented objectives of reinforcement learning. By highlighting the limitations of the standard pre-training pipeline—where a VAE is trained in isolation on a static dataset—the authors convincingly demonstrate that such approaches often neglect task-relevant features, like small visual details in Atari, due to their negligible impact on reconstruction error. The introduction is articulate and effectively motivates the need for joint training strategies to embed RL-specific information directly into the VAE latent space. However, a significant issue for the reader at this stage is that the paper has not yet disclosed the specific architectures or training algorithms proposed to integrate policy gradients into the VAE; the focus thus far is almost entirely on identifying the problem rather than explaining the solution.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology between text and figure caption
comments.quote: We can clearly see, that the Jacobian has high values at missing blocks as well as around the ball...
comments.explanation: The text refers to the Jacobian highlighting 'missing blocks,' which is ambiguous. It could refer to blocks that are physically absent from the game (empty space) or blocks missing from the VAE reconstruction. The caption for Figure 4 explicitly states the highlighted areas are 'around the ball and the blocks,' referring to the active game elements. Given the context of the paper discussing task-relevant features (bricks and ball), 'missing blocks' in the text appears to be a typo for 'reconstructed blocks' or simply 'blocks,' creating confusion about what features are being emphasized.

comments.title: Grammatical error and misplaced modifier in visualization description
comments.quote: Only visualizing above-mean Jacobian values removes some noise in the blue channel makes the images much easier to interpret
comments.explanation: The sentence structure is broken and grammatically awkward. It combines the action of adding values with a condition and a consequence, resulting in a clause that reads: 'Only visualizing above-mean Jacobian values removes some noise in the blue channel makes the images much easier to interpret.' The phrase 'removes some noise in the blue channel' appears to be a misplaced fragment intended to clarify the method, but it disrupts the grammatical flow and clarity of the statement.

comments.quote: added the values of the Jacobian to the blue channel if the were greater than the mean value of the Jacobian.
comments.explanation: There is a typo where 'the' should be 'they'. This is a minor typographical error that does not affect understanding but should be corrected for readability.
